**Developing the React App**

I'm currently focused on the framework of the React SPA, specifically designing the high-end portfolio and landing page functionalities for 3D and premium web development services. I'm exploring layout options and React component structures to provide a compelling and user-friendly experience that reflects the high-quality nature of the services.


**Converting Design Concepts**

I'm currently translating the sandbox-style visual design and content requests into a proper React application structure. Adapting the vanilla JS specifications into React components is the primary focus. Specifically, I'm integrating the ultra-modern aesthetic and 3D elements detailed in the prompt. I'm prioritizing the use of React Three Fiber for 3D elements, as it aligns better with the React ecosystem. Additionally, I'm planning to implement the layout (Hero, Features, Portfolio sections) while ensuring compatibility with React's component-based nature. I'll utilize the provided libraries (GSAP, Three.js) in a React-friendly manner, like using `gsap.to` within `useEffect` hooks for animations, and handling Three.js setup/teardown correctly.


**Reconciling Library Choices**

I'm currently addressing the integration of `three.js` with React. While `react-three-fiber` is the standard, the prompt specifies `three.js`, so I'll wrap the logic within a component to meet the user's request. Also, I'm opting for a `locomotive-scroll` wrapper, keeping it compatible within a React structure.


**Adjusting Scroll Implementation**

I'm reassessing the scroll-related libraries. Although the prompt requests `locomotive-scroll`, its integration is proving problematic in a clean React environment, especially with the v4 CDN version. I'm now leaning towards `lenis` or `ScrollSmoother` using GSAP for a smoother React experience.  I've found a better path that leans on `tsparticles` as a replacement for particles.js. Furthermore, I've confirmed that `locomotive-scroll` v5 is vanilla JS, so this simplifies things. I will be working with standard `npm` packages within the app due to runtime and system constraints.


**Adding Gemini Features**

I'm now integrating the Gemini features, as the prompt specifies this as a "must-add" element. I'm focusing on the "Gemini intelligence" for content analysis and edits, image generation using Nano Banana Pro, a chatbot, and fast responses.  These are injected as supplementary functionalities *within* the context of the requested premium landing page.  I'm interpreting the "Prompt Completo" as the core landing page design, and these Gemini features as enhancements. This means implementing the requested app as a premium landing page first, and then adding features later.


**Adding Gemini Section**

I've added the new Gemini section, now known as the "Live AI Demo." This incorporates the chatbot and image generator requested, built using the Gemini API. This approach aligns the demo with a portfolio aesthetic, showcasing the integrated AI features. I'm focusing on user-friendly interfaces for both the chatbot and image generation, ensuring they are intuitive and responsive within the React environment.


**Optimizing UI Integration**

I'm now integrating the requested libraries in a React-friendly manner, ensuring that the components function cohesively. I'm focusing on UI integration of the Gemini features, especially the sticky chatbot button. I'm prioritizing user experience and efficient rendering.


**Planning Aesthetics & Features**

I'm now prioritizing the aesthetics and features to add. For the AI demo, I'm thinking of "Experience our Design AI" for image generation and a "Magic Copywriter" tool for fast response. The default dark mode with Indigo to Pink gradients and Inter/Space Grotesk fonts will enhance the modern look. I plan to load fonts via Google Fonts.


**Building Out Components**

I'm now focusing on implementing the requested components. I'll translate the 3D Hero section using `TorusKnotGeometry` and `MeshNormalMaterial`, incorporating mouse-move rotation. Since `SplitText` is a paid GSAP plugin, I'll manually split text for the animations to avoid potential conflicts in the generated environment. I'm also planning to use GSAP ScrollTrigger for scroll-based interactions. I will be incorporating the file list into my workflow. For the particle effect, I've decided on `tsparticles` as a replacement for particles.js.



**Enhancing Particle System**

I'm now prioritizing performance and visual coherence. I've decided to implement a custom particle system using Three.js' `PointsMaterial` directly within the Hero scene. This approach aligns better with the 3D aesthetic and offers improved performance compared to a DOM-based particle library.



**Initiating Coding Phase**

I'm now starting the coding phase, building upon the planned components. I'll begin with the 3D Hero section, implementing the `PointsMaterial`-based particle system for an efficient and visually appealing effect. The custom particle system directly uses Three.js' capabilities and the file list to maintain visual coherence and performance. I'm focusing on the first prototype of this section.

Dar para melhorar, as transições entre as sessões ainda estão padrões e muito secas.

Quero que até na troca de sessões tenha o efeito de profundidade. utilizando o proprio objeto animado do hero.

Quero que os textos vai aparecendo conforme for escrolando com efeito de profundidade, e quanto mais escrollando mais vai dando imersão de que você esta entrando dentro da sessão e não como se fosse apenas rolando para baixo, é como se fosse um hero infinito, que todo o conteudo estivesse dentro do hero e conforme escrolamos, vai aparecendo o resto do conteudo e o conteudo anterior passando pela tela e ficando para tras como se estivessemos atravessando. 

Crie dados ficticios para a landing page ficar mais completa e adicione mais transições com objetos 3d e troque também o nome lumina para onzy se possivel utilize a mesma fonte

**Redefining Immersive Transitions**

I'm now focusing on how the "infinite hero" feel could be implemented by analyzing the user's request to make section transitions more engaging. I'm exploring methods to achieve a continuous, immersive 3D experience, examining the core mechanic, and brainstorming less "dry" alternatives to current transition methods. The goal is to create a seamless, flowing experience for the user.


**Analyzing Fly-Through Effect**

I've been drilling down on the "infinite fly-through" effect. The plan now centers on a fixed canvas with scrolling HTML that appears to fly past the camera via GSAP and ScrollTrigger animations. I'm focusing on aggressive scaling, opacity, and Z-axis transformations to generate depth. I am updating 3D elements to react to user scrolling for a more dynamic experience.


**Developing Depth Perception**

I'm now implementing the core mechanics for the "warp speed" effect. The plan is for background elements, like stars, to move faster based on scroll. This will give the illusion of depth. I am also planning to change the central geometry based on section, e.g. Torus to Icosahedron to Sphere. I'm focusing on CSS 3D transforms for sections. This involves perspective and translateZ animations.


**Enhancing 3D Immersion**

I'm now integrating scroll-driven morphing and background "Starfield" effects within the Three.js scene, aiming for a dynamic "flying in" sensation. I am focusing on making the transitions visually compelling. I'm focusing on scroll-based logic to morph shapes and manage background movement to achieve this. My current plan involves refactoring the app layout, introducing `perspective: 1000px` for the main container, and refining section transitions using GSAP and ScrollTrigger, specifically animating `z` and `opacity`. The goal is to create overlapping sections with a distinct sense of depth, with one section scaling up and fading out as the next one fades in from a smaller scale.


**Implementing Shape Transformations**

I'm now focused on the "flying through" sensation. I'm exploring the best way to do this. A tunnel of particles is the current frontrunner, with a changing central object. I'm moving away from basic geometry morphing because it's tricky. Instead, I'll use a `Group` containing rotating/scaling shapes based on scroll.  I'm exploring a GSAP pinning pattern and animating scale and blur on sections. The hero section will scale up toward the camera while fading out.


**Optimizing 3D Integration**

I'm now refining the Three.js integration, focusing on a fixed `ThreeHero` component that covers the entire viewport. Sections will feature transparent backgrounds or glassmorphism to showcase the 3D element. I'm prioritizing adding a "Warp Drive" particle effect and having the central shape evolve through sections, starting with a Torus. The objective is to ensure that the 3D element is the centerpiece. My thought process is now on how to transition between section geometry and content.


**Defining App Structure**

I'm now deep-diving into the final code architecture. I've got the `App.tsx` file for implementing the "Fly Through" scroll effect, rebranding updates, and content expansion, plus updates to the `ThreeHero.tsx` component, including fixing the background and integrating ScrollTrigger for camera movement and shape morphing. I'm focusing on key element interactions, like updating `metadata.json` for name change and `services/geminiService.ts` for a system prompt update. The plan is clear, now for execution.


**Executing Scroll-Triggered Transitions**

I'm now focusing on refining the scroll-triggered transitions within `ThreeHero`. I'll address any context-related issues to ensure `ScrollTrigger` functions correctly. My plan is to thoroughly review the integration of `ScrollTrigger` and Three.js elements. My current plan involves detailed testing and debugging to create a seamless user experience. I'm focusing on ensuring smooth camera movements and shape morphing in response to scroll events.

Here is the complete transformation of the app to "Onzy".

**Key Changes:**
1.  **Immersive 3D Background (`ThreeHero.tsx`):** The 3D scene is now fixed in the background. As you scroll, the camera physically moves through a particle field ("Warp Speed" effect), and the central geometry morphs from a Torus (Hero) to a Cube (Features) to a Sphere (Portfolio) based on the scroll position.
2.  **Fly-Through HTML Transitions (`App.tsx`):** I've applied a "Z-Axis" animation to the sections. As you scroll down, the current section scales UP (flying towards the camera) and fades out, while the next section fades in from the background. This creates the illusion of moving *into* the website.
3.  **Rebranding:** Everything is now "Onzy", keeping the same typography.

Quero que você gere um efeito de imersão com scroll, funcionando como se o usuário estivesse se movendo para frente dentro de um espaço 3D.
Não quero que o scroll mova a página para baixo.
Quero que o scroll controle a câmera, aplicando zoom e profundidade, fazendo os elementos “passarem por mim”.

🎯 COMPORTAMENTO EXATO QUE PRECISO
1. Hero em tela cheia

O Hero deve ocupar 100vh.

O scroll NÃO deve descer para outra seção.

O scroll deve ser interceptado e transformado em movimento 3D.

2. Animação ao scrollar

Quando o usuário começar a scrollar:

📌 Textos na frente

Os textos do hero devem se aproximar rapidamente da câmera como se viessem em minha direção.

Depois devem atravessar completamente a tela (saindo por trás da câmera).

Após atravessarem, devem sumir suavemente (fade-out ou ocultação automática).

📌 Objeto 3D (já existente no fundo)

O objeto 3D deve começar distante.

À medida que o usuário scrolla:

Ele deve vir se aproximando da câmera, como se eu estivesse entrando nele.

Quando estiver bem próximo, deve “envolver” a tela.

Depois devo atravessar através do objeto, continuando o movimento.

Quando eu terminar de atravessar ele, a próxima sessão aparece.

3. Sessão seguinte

A próxima sessão deve surgir automaticamente quando o objeto 3D já foi totalmente ultrapassado.

Nada deve deslizar para cima — tudo deve ocorrer com movimento 3D, câmera e profundidade.

**Examining Immersiveness**

I've been drilling into the core goal: building this immersive fly-through. The initial analysis locked in on the "no vertical scrolling" constraint, which forces all motion to be scroll-controlled. I'm now weighing the best implementation strategies, considering both performance and user experience. My focus is on how to make the user feel like they're actually flying.


**Analyzing Interactions**

I'm now diving into the specifics of the hero section interaction. I want to emphasize the depth; text should move towards the camera, through it, and fade, while the 3D object envelopes the user, creating a feeling of passing through. This section will be in Portuguese, and I'll leverage the existing React, Three.js, GSAP, and Tailwind stack. To achieve this, I'll use GSAP's ScrollTrigger with `pin: true` to pin the entire content and trigger camera and object animations as the user scrolls. My focus will be on seamless camera movement and smooth object alignment within the 3D scene.


**Developing the Animation**

I'm now refining the animation details. The infinite tunnel particles are in place, but the camera needs a considerable forward movement. I'm restructuring the DOM elements from relative positioning to fixed or absolute, stacking them in Z-space. CSS perspective will be crucial for making these HTML elements behave like 3D objects. A single master GSAP timeline will be linked to the main container's scroll. From 0-20% scroll, the hero text will scale up (Z-translate) and fade out. Then, from 20-60%, the 3D object will approach and pass the camera. Finally, from 60-100%, the next section will fade in and scale down from "behind" the camera to its normal position. To create the illusion of no vertical scrolling, the body height will be significantly increased, and the content container will be fixed.


**Refining Implementation**

I've been working on integrating the `ThreeHero` component with the broader scroll-driven layout. After some deliberation, I decided to keep the core `ThreeHero` logic self-contained but synchronized with the "pinned" layout. I'm focused on achieving the "flying text" effect using CSS `translateZ` and GSAP's `z` property to create depth. My strategy is to use a 500vh wrapper for scroll space, a sticky container for the hero section, and absolute-positioned sections within. The core of my current focus is on the camera movement relative to the Torus animation, ensuring that the Torus comes very close before fading.


**Redrafting the Timeline**

I'm now overhauling the timeline logic.  The structural changes to `App.tsx` are in progress, transitioning to the Wrapper -> Sticky -> Absolute Sections layout. The goal is now a continuous timeline driven by scroll, rather than section-specific triggers.  I'm drafting the steps, starting with CSS perspective and layers, then synchronizing the camera's Z position with scroll.  The App logic will use `useLayoutEffect` for GSAP. I'm focusing on a 3-part animation: hero text flying, the 3D object passing the camera, and the final state.


**Adjusting Camera Movement**

I'm now revising my camera movement strategy. The current focus is on the `camera.position.z` value to move it *past* the Torus Knot, maintaining the immersive "fly-through" feel. I've been experimenting with ways to smoothly decrease the `z` position. I'm prioritizing ensuring the user feels a sense of speed and momentum as they approach and pass the 3D object. I've considered and discarded other geometries in favor of the existing Torus Knot, focusing on the camera's path to achieve the desired effect.


**Prioritizing Smooth Transitions**

I'm now zeroing in on the Hero-to-About transition. The plan is to have a ScrollTrigger pin the entire app for the sequence. I'll focus on a pure Z-scroll effect for the entrance, ensuring that the camera smoothly glides through the 3D ring, transitioning into the "About" section. After that, I will either release the pin or continue a Z-pattern if requested.


**Integrating the Layout**

I'm now adapting the layout to the Z-axis stacking strategy for the entire page, which best fits the project's theme. All sections will be stacked, with scroll position determining the visible one. This requires updates to `ThreeHero.tsx` to handle scroll progress and update `App.tsx` with a `h-[500vh]` scrollable container, a sticky content container, and absolute-positioned, initially hidden sections. The GSAP timeline will be revised to control the transitions between these sections, from hero text zoom to section zoom-ins. The `ThreeHero` component will be updated to accept scroll progress.

**Refining Implementation**

I'm now focusing on ensuring that the torus rotation is correct so the hole in the center actually faces the camera. I need to get the "flying through" effect right, so the rotation of the torus is very important. I will update `ThreeHero` with a `useLayoutEffect` to bind GSAP and the camera Z position. Finally, I need to make the texts Portuguese.


**Implementing Pointer Interactions**

I'm now integrating pointer interaction management to enhance the user experience. I've realized I need to control `pointer-events` on sections; when a section is active, it will use `pointer-events: auto`, and when it flies away, it will use `pointer-events: none`. My focus is on ensuring smooth transitions and preventing unintended interactions during animation. I'm actively working to ensure the user can seamlessly navigate the immersive experience.

To achieve this specific **"Fly-Through"** behavior where scrolling controls the camera depth (Z-axis) rather than moving the page down, we need to completely restructure the layout engine.

Here is the plan:
1.  **Architecture:** We will use a "Virtual Scroll" technique. The body will be very tall (`500vh`+) to allow scrolling, but the content container will be `fixed` at `100vh`.
2.  **App.tsx (The Stage):** We will stack all sections (Hero, About, Features, etc.) on top of each other using `position: absolute`. GSAP will control their `opacity`, `scale`, and `z-index` based on scroll progress.
3.  **ThreeHero.tsx (The Engine):** The 3D camera will physically move forward on the Z-axis synced with the scroll. The Torus object will be positioned so that we fly *through* its center hole.

Here are the changes:

# Updated files:

## components/ThreeHero.tsx

**Changes:**
*   Replaced the rotation animation with a specific **Camera Z-Axis Movement**.
*   Rotated the Torus geometry to face the camera (so we can fly through the hole).
*   Synced the 3D movement to the global scroll trigger. When you scroll, the camera moves from `z=30` to `z=-50`, effectively passing through the objects.
*   Added logic to "explode" or fade the object as the camera passes through to prevent clipping artifacts.

## App.tsx

**Changes:**
*   **Structural Overhaul:** Removed the standard flex/block layout. Created a `h-[600vh]` "ghost" container to provide scroll track, while the actual content is a `fixed inset-0` container.
*   **Z-Axis Navigation:** All sections are now stacked absolutely.
*   **GSAP Timeline:**
    *   **Step 1:** Hero Text scales up massively (fly towards user) and fades out.
    *   **Step 2:** The "camera" (visually) passes through the 3D void.
    *   **Step 3:** The "About" section fades in from the distance (`scale: 0.5` -> `1`).
    *   **Step 4:** Subsequent sections follow this "fly-in/fly-out" pattern.
*   **Pointer Events:** Logic added to ensure you can only click buttons on the *active* section.

entries.description: Implement true 3D camera fly-through logic synced with scroll, orienting geometry for tunnel effect.
Total layout restructure: Fixed container with absolute sections controlled by Z-axis transforms via GSAP.

Landing page 3D premium que usa scroll cinematográfico para criar jornada imersiva. Cada seção revela-se através de movimento de câmera e profundidade, não scroll tradicional. O site É o portfólio - demonstra as capacidades enquanto vende o serviço.

---

"Armados com o Futuro"

### Processo Dimensional:
```javascript
// Timeline se desenha progressivamente
gsap.to(".process-line", {
  scrollTrigger: {
    trigger: ".process-section",
    start: "top center",
    end: "bottom center",
    scrub: 1
  },
  strokeDashoffset: 0
});

// Cards surgem em sequência
gsap.from(".process-card", {
  scrollTrigger: ".process-section",
  y: 100,
  opacity: 0,
  scale: 0.8,
  stagger: 0.3,
  duration: 0.8
});
```

### Arsenal Tecnológico:
```javascript
// Grid surge com efeito cascata
gsap.from(".tech-card", {
  scrollTrigger: {
    trigger: ".arsenal-section",
    start: "top 80%"
  },
  scale: 0,
  opacity: 0,
  rotation: 180,
  stagger: {
    amount: 1.2,
    from: "center"
  }
});
```

### Métricas:
```javascript
// Contadores animados
document.querySelectorAll('.metric-number').forEach(el => {
  const target = el.dataset.target;
  gsap.to(el, {
    scrollTrigger: el,
    textContent: target,
    duration: 2,
    snap: { textContent: 1 },
    ease: "power1.out"
  });
});
```

**Refining the Approach**

**Defining Interactivity Goals**

It's really exciting to envision the "Process in 4 Dimensions" timeline with progressive reveals and animated connecting lines. The "Numbers that Speak" metrics section will feature scroll-driven counters and 3D hover effects. For "Armed with the Future," I'm aiming for a floating, connector-line experience with an asymmetric layout. Furthermore, I will create independent text animations and refine the camera logic to provide a great experience on mobile and desktop devices.


**Exploring ScrollTrigger Options**

I'm now revisiting the scroll trigger strategy, realizing standard implementations won't work with the virtual scroll setup. I'm choosing between two approaches: managing component animations from `App.tsx` using refs, or attaching `ScrollTrigger` instances to the `.scroll-track` and mapping ranges. A third, more refined strategy is also emerging: rewriting `App.tsx` to manage the "Global Camera" and delegate local animations with timelines or `scrollTrigger` ranges. Alternatively, I will utilize `gsap.fromTo` inside the components.


**Defining Component Animation Strategy**

I'm now thinking about how to best coordinate animations across components. A more modular approach is emerging: managing the fly-through animation in `App.tsx` and triggering internal component animations based on section visibility. This involves `App.tsx` controlling the overall camera movement and delegating animation responsibilities to each section, allowing each to handle its own specific visual effects and interactions. Specifically, I'll refine the animation and layout within sections such as Process, Metrics, and TechStack.


**Initiating the Build**

I'm now in the process of implementing the animations, starting with adding the CSS classes. I'm focusing on incorporating glassmorphism, gradients, and animated lines. Next, I will refactor the components for their individual animations, then integrate them with the master scroll timeline. The orchestration in `App.tsx` will be crucial for the smooth, sequenced transitions I'm aiming for.


**Sequencing Animations Precisely**

I'm now focusing on fine-tuning the animation sequences, specifically within the "Process" and "Tech Stack" sections. The "Process" section's timeline requires the title to fade in, the line to grow, and the cards to slide in sequentially. For "Tech Stack," I'm implementing responsive positioning, using `md:absolute` for desktops and `relative` for mobile to ensure proper stacking. I'm also revising the scroll logic in `App.tsx` to insert specific tweens during the section's "Hold" phase, ensuring internal animations align with the timeline's duration and section visibility.


**Integrating Camera Simulation**

I am now focusing on implementing the "camera descends" effect for the Process section. I'll simulate Y-axis movement by animating the `.process-container`'s `y` value as the master timeline progresses, creating the desired "camera pan" effect. **Addressing Visual Details**

I'm now integrating the requested visual details. I've imported `TextPlugin` to manage text animations, but I might use a fallback if needed. I'm focusing on making the timeline construction modular. I'm building staggered entrances and vertical pans for the "Process" section. The metrics section is now counting up with 3D hover effects. The "Tech Stack" section features an asymmetric pop-in and floating animations. I've added independent text animations with `gsap.from`. I am also working on edge cases, such as mobile layout of the "TechStack" section.


**Optimizing Layout Responsiveness**

I've finally landed on the best approach for responsive positioning, especially with the "TechStack" section. I've decided to leverage Tailwind's `md:absolute` and `md:left-[x]` classes for desktop layouts, while reverting to static positioning on mobile. This addresses the mobile edge case concerns.
